prompt: Which detail best supports the claim that a kelp forest can collapse quickly?

choices.text: the statement that without otters, urchins multiply and graze the kelp to bare rock
choices.rationale: Correct. Urchins grazing the kelp to bare rock directly shows a collapse.

choices.text: the statement that the kelp can grow half a meter in a single day
choices.rationale: Fast growth describes vigor, not collapse.

choices.text: the statement that giant kelp can stretch dozens of meters toward the surface
choices.rationale: The kelp's height describes its size, not a collapse.

choices.text: the statement that kelp forests shelter fish, snails, and otters
choices.rationale: Sheltering animals describes richness, not collapse.

explanation: Collapse is supported by the urchins-graze-to-bare-rock detail.

stimulus.title: Forests Beneath the Waves
stimulus.text: Off some cool, rocky coasts grow forests that no hiker will ever walk through. Giant kelp, anchored to the seafloor, can stretch dozens of meters toward the surface, swaying in the current like trees in wind. These kelp forests shelter fish, snails, and otters, much as a woodland shelters birds and deer. The kelp grows astonishingly fast, sometimes half a meter in a single day, drinking sunlight near the surface. But the forest is fragile. Where otters vanish, sea urchins multiply and graze the kelp down to bare rock, leaving a barren the divers call an urchin desert. A single missing animal can unmake a whole forest.

prompt: Which detail best supports the claim that some folk weather signs had real value?

choices.text: the statement that the conditions producing a hazy moon truly can precede rain
choices.rationale: Correct. The hazy-moon-precedes-rain detail shows a folk sign with a real basis.

choices.text: the statement that satellites now map storms from orbit
choices.rationale: Satellite mapping describes modern tools, not folk-sign value.

choices.text: the statement that much of the folk knowledge mixed in superstition
choices.rationale: The superstition detail shows the unreliable side, not the value.

choices.text: the statement that such clues were gathered into almanacs and passed down
choices.rationale: Gathering clues into almanacs concerns how they spread, not their accuracy.

explanation: Real value is supported by the hazy-moon-precedes-rain detail.

stimulus.title: Reading the Weather Before Radio
stimulus.text: Long before satellites mapped storms from orbit, farmers learned to read the sky and the land for signs of coming weather. A ring of haze around the moon, the smell of soil before rain, the behavior of swallows flying low: such clues were gathered into almanacs and passed down through families. Much of this folk knowledge was unreliable, mixing keen observation with superstition. Yet some of it held real wisdom, since the conditions that produce a hazy moon truly can precede rain. Modern forecasting has not so much replaced this older watching as absorbed its best parts, trading the rooster's crow for the radar's sweep.

prompt: Which detail best supports the claim that some seeds rely on animals eating them?

choices.text: the statement that some seeds grow papery wings and spin on the wind
choices.rationale: Winged seeds rely on wind, not on being eaten.

choices.text: the statement that some seeds wear hooks that snag passing fur
choices.rationale: Hooked seeds rely on fur, not on being eaten.

choices.text: the statement that a plant cannot walk to find open ground
choices.rationale: The opening notes a plant cannot walk, unrelated to being eaten.

choices.text: the statement that some seeds hide in sweet fruit so an animal eats and carries them off
choices.rationale: Correct. Hiding in fruit to be eaten and carried directly supports the claim.

explanation: Reliance on being eaten is supported by the sweet-fruit detail.

stimulus.title: Travelers Without Legs
stimulus.text: A plant cannot walk, yet its seeds must somehow leave the shadow of the parent and find open ground. Evolution has answered this need with a surprising range of tricks. Some seeds grow papery wings and spin away on the wind like tiny helicopters. Others wear hooks that snag the fur of passing animals, hitching a ride for miles. Still others hide inside sweet fruit, counting on an animal to eat them and carry them off, depositing them far away. Each method is a kind of bargain struck with the world around the plant. The seed that travels farthest is often the one with the best chance to grow where the parent's shade cannot reach.

prompt: Which detail best supports the claim that inconsistent local time could be dangerous?

choices.text: the statement that each town set its time by the sun overhead at noon
choices.rationale: Setting time by the sun describes the old method, not the danger.

choices.text: the statement that a traveler adjusted a watch town by town
choices.rationale: Adjusting a watch shows inconvenience, not danger.

choices.text: the statement that people grumbled about clocks no longer matching the sun
choices.rationale: Grumbling about clocks concerns reaction, not danger.

choices.text: the statement that a missed connection could be a matter of life and death
choices.rationale: Correct. 'A matter of life and death' directly shows the danger of inconsistent time.

explanation: The danger is supported by the 'life and death' detail.

stimulus.title: When Noon Moved
stimulus.text: Before railroads, every town kept its own time, set by the sun overhead at noon. A traveler crossing the country adjusted a watch town by town, and no one minded a few minutes' difference. Railroads made the patchwork intolerable. Trains ran on schedules, and a schedule means nothing if each station defines noon differently; a missed connection could be a matter of life and death. So the railways carved the land into broad zones, each agreeing to share a single time. People grumbled at first about clocks that no longer matched the sun, but the convenience won out. The hour on the wall had become a public agreement rather than a private reading of the sky.

prompt: Which detail best supports the claim that a delta depends on continuing sediment delivery?

choices.text: the statement that when dams trap sediment, the delta is starved and the coast retreats
choices.rationale: Correct. The dam-and-retreat detail directly shows the delta's dependence on sediment supply.

choices.text: the statement that a river carries soil scoured from distant hills
choices.rationale: Carrying soil explains the source, not the dependence over time.

choices.text: the statement that the river slows where it finally meets the sea
choices.rationale: The river slowing explains why sediment settles, not the ongoing dependence.

choices.text: the statement that a delta can fan into a maze of channels and islands
choices.rationale: The maze of channels describes shape, not the supply dependence.

explanation: Dependence on sediment is supported by the dam-starvation detail.

stimulus.title: Land Made of Mud
stimulus.text: A great river carries more than water; it carries soil, scoured from distant hills and ground into fine particles. Where the river finally slows and meets the sea, it can no longer hold this load, and the sediment settles out, building flat new land called a delta. Over centuries a delta can grow seaward, fanning into a maze of channels and islands. But the process depends on a steady supply of mud. When dams upstream trap that sediment, the delta is starved; waves carry off more land than the river replaces, and the coastline begins to retreat. What looks like permanent ground is, in truth, a balance constantly being rebuilt.

prompt: Which detail best supports the idea that Mr. Owusu paid close attention to individual customers?

choices.text: the detail that the market felt larger after he retired
choices.rationale: The market feeling larger is the effect of his absence, not his attention.

choices.text: the detail that he recalled which customer's child disliked onions
choices.rationale: Correct. Remembering a specific child's dislike shows close attention to individuals.

choices.text: the detail that his stall was, for years, just one among many
choices.rationale: Being one stall among many does not show attentiveness.

choices.text: the detail that he set out the same vegetables every Saturday
choices.rationale: Setting out the same vegetables shows routine, not personal attention.

explanation: Close personal attention is shown by his recalling the child's dislike of onions.

stimulus.title: The Stall That Listened
stimulus.text: Every Saturday, Mr. Owusu set out the same vegetables on the same table, and for years his stall was just one among many. What set him apart, his daughter noticed, was not his prices but his memory. He recalled which customer's child disliked onions, who was cooking for a crowd that weekend, who had been ill and needed something gentle. He would set aside a perfect tomato for one regular and toss an extra handful of beans to another. People did not return only for the produce; they returned because, at his stall, they were known. When he finally retired, the market felt larger and lonelier, though not a single other stall had closed.

prompt: Which detail best supports the claim that ice cores capture air from the distant past?

choices.text: the statement that each winter's snowfall does not entirely melt
choices.rationale: Snow not melting explains how layers form, not the captured air.

choices.text: the statement that the bubbles hold the very atmosphere that existed when the snow fell
choices.rationale: Correct. The bubbles holding the atmosphere of when the snow fell directly captures past air.

choices.text: the statement that the ice keeps no diary of dates
choices.rationale: Keeping 'no diary of dates' concerns dating, not capturing old air.

choices.text: the statement that drilling deep is a way of drilling back through time
choices.rationale: The 'drilling back through time' image is a metaphor, less direct than the bubble detail.

explanation: Capturing past air is supported by the trapped-atmosphere detail.

stimulus.title: Bubbles of Old Air
stimulus.text: Each winter's snowfall on a polar ice sheet does not entirely melt; it packs down, traps tiny pockets of air, and slowly turns to ice. Layer settles on layer, year after year, so that drilling deep into the ice is a way of drilling back through time. Trapped in those bubbles is a sample of the very atmosphere that existed when the snow fell, including the gases it carried. By analyzing cores pulled from kilometers down, researchers can compare today's air with the air of tens of thousands of years ago. The ice keeps no diary of dates, yet its layers, counted carefully, let scientists assign an age to each whisper of ancient air.

prompt: Which detail best supports the claim that public voting could intimidate voters?

choices.text: the statement that reformers pushed for a printed ballot dropped in a box
choices.rationale: The reformers' push is the response, not evidence of intimidation.

choices.text: the statement that a worker might think twice before voting against a watching boss
choices.rationale: Correct. The hesitant worker example directly shows intimidation under public voting.

choices.text: the statement that it is easy to assume voting has always been private
choices.rationale: The opening assumption does not show intimidation.

choices.text: the statement that the change shifted power toward the ordinary voter
choices.rationale: The power shift is the effect of reform, not the intimidation itself.

explanation: Intimidation is supported by the watching-boss example.

stimulus.title: The Secret Ballot
stimulus.text: It is easy to assume voting has always been private, but for a long time it was not. In many places people voted aloud or by a show of hands, in full view of employers, landlords, and neighbors. That openness invited pressure: a worker might think twice before voting against a boss who could see the choice. Reformers pushed for a printed ballot, marked alone and dropped in a box, so that no one need fear retaliation for an honest vote. The change seemed minor, a mere matter of paper, yet it shifted real power toward the ordinary voter, who could now decide without an audience.
